data.title: American Water Works, Essential Utilities merger approved by Kentucky regulator
data.description:
American Water Works (AWK) and Essential Utilities (WTRG) said Wednesday that the Kentucky Public Service Commission approved the companies' proposed merger.

The Kentucky PSC's approval follows the merger's approval by shareholders of both companies at their respective special shareholder meetings held in February.

The all-stock transaction, announced in October, will create a combined company serving more than 4.7M water and wastewater customer connections and more than 740K gas customer connections across 17 states and on 18 military installations.

The combined company has said it expects to maintain American Water's (AWK) 7%-9% earnings per share and dividend growth targets.

The company will operate under the American Water (AWK) name and be headquartered in Camden, New Jersey.

data.title: Navy Secretary John Phelan ousted amid clash with Pentagon leadership
data.description:
John Phelan has been removed as secretary of the Navy following months of internal friction with Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, according to U.S. officials familiar with the matter.

The leadership change was disclosed in a social media statement from Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell, who said Hegseth and Deputy Defense Secretary Steve Feinberg were “grateful to Secretary Phelan for his service to the Department and the United States Navy.”

Parnell said Hung Cao, the current undersecretary of the Navy and a military veteran, will assume the role on an acting basis. Cao was the Republican nominee in Virginia’s 2024 Senate race, where he was defeated by Democratic Sen. Tim Kaine.

Phelan’s departure comes as U.S. forces are carrying out a large naval containment effort around Iranian ports, with more than 15 American warships operating in the Middle East region.

Officials said Phelan’s tenure had been marked by a strained relationship with Hegseth and Feinberg, including resentment over Phelan’s direct access to President Donald Trump, The Wall Street Journal reported.

Phelan, who lives near Trump’s Mar-a-Lago property in Florida, was known to speak regularly with the president and previously told lawmakers he exchanged late-night messages with Trump about naval shipbuilding.

According to people familiar with internal discussions, tensions worsened last fall after Phelan proposed a new battleship concept directly to Trump without first going through Hegseth, the Journal reported.

In the months that followed, Pentagon leadership moved to reduce Phelan’s influence, officials said, including the creation of a new submarine acquisition overseer reporting directly to Feinberg rather than through traditional Navy channels.

The episode adds to broader turmoil within the Pentagon. Hegseth has dismissed nearly two dozen senior military officers during his tenure, including Army Chief of Staff Gen. Randy George, and has also been at odds with senior Army leadership.

The removal of Phelan comes during the Navy’s largest annual conference, where he had spent recent days outlining service priorities to defense contractors and journalists. It also follows the Pentagon’s release of its latest budget proposal to Congress, which includes $65.8 billion earmarked for shipbuilding.

data.title: Crown Castle reiterates 2026 outlook with ~$1B buyback plan and $65M annualized cost reduction
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Crown Castle (CCI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered solid first quarter results and are reiterating our guidance for full year 2026." (CEO, President & Director Christian Hillabrant)

  • "This is a transformative year for Crown Castle, and we believe we have an opportunity to generate attractive shareholder returns as we transition to a stand-alone tower business and pursue our goal of becoming a best-in-class U.S. tower operator." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • "Our first priority is to conclude the sale of our small cell and fiber businesses, which we believe remains on track to close in the first half of 2026." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • "After DISH defaulted on its payment obligations in January, we exercised our right to terminate the agreement, and we are seeking to recover the remaining payments DISH owed for the terms of the contract." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • "During the first quarter, we amended our pending litigation against DISH to include a claim for breach of contract alongside our request for declaratory judgment." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • "In the first quarter, we successfully executed a restructuring of our tower and corporate organizations, resulting in an anticipated $65 million reduction to annualized run rate cost." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • "First quarter organic growth, excluding the impact of Sprint cancellations and DISH terminations, was 3.1% or $30 million" and was "more than offset at site rental revenues by $5 million of Sprint cancellations, $49 million of DISH terminations and a $26 million decrease in noncash straight-line revenues and amortization of prepaid rent." (Executive VP & CFO Sunit Patel)

Outlook

  • "Our full year outlook remains unchanged." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "At the midpoint of the range for full year 2026, we expect site rental revenues of approximately $3.9 billion, adjusted EBITDA of approximately $2.7 billion and AFFO of approximately $1.9 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "As a reminder, for the purposes of building our full year 2026 outlook, we'll assume the sale of the small cell and fiber businesses closes on June 30." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "Following the close of the transaction, we plan to allocate approximately $1 billion to share repurchases and approximately $7 billion to repay debt, allowing us to remain at our target leverage range between 6x and 6.5x." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "Our full year 2026 outlook positions us well to meet our unchanged range for AFFO for the 12 months following the anticipated close of the transaction of $2.1 billion at the midpoint." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted EBITDA and AFFO in the first quarter benefited from lower repair and maintenance costs, sustaining capital expenditures and other nonlabor costs" but "these lower costs were largely due to timing and seasonality, so we expect them to occur later in the year." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "We also experienced a modest decrease in quarterly interest expense due to lower-than-anticipated short-term borrowing rates." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • "Lastly, our outlook for discretionary CapEx remains unchanged at $200 million or $160 million, net of $40 million of prepaid rent received." (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

Q&A

  • Ric Prentiss, Raymond James: asked about potentially splitting the fiber/small cell transaction approvals to accelerate closing; CEO Hillabrant: "we remain extremely confident that we will close by the end of first half or as soon as possible."

  • Ric Prentiss, Raymond James: asked whether CCI’s medium/long-term growth should differ from peers; CEO Hillabrant: "nothing systemically" and the difference has been "the timing of when that growth occurred," adding the portfolio skews "towards urban and suburban" which can drive "earlier in the cycle" activity.

  • Matthew Niknam, Truist: asked about 5G densification and whether satellite partnerships affect planning; CEO Hillabrant: densification behavior is "not really any change from past deployments" and satellite impact is "de minimis or inconsequential at this point."

  • Aryeh Klein, BMO: asked where benchmarking vs peers can drive improvement; CEO Hillabrant: the goal is improving customer experience so that "when there's choice of a customer between 2 tower companies that we win 100% of the jump balls."

  • Richard Choe, JPMorgan: asked about timing and meaning of edge compute; CEO Hillabrant: "I would characterize this in the trial phase" and said the company is pursuing it "with very little capital required," while noting most sites have fiber backhaul.

  • Eric Luebchow, Wells Fargo: asked about new tower builds and margin opportunity; CEO Hillabrant: "I certainly don't want to raise expectations that we're going into a mass tower build here" and it will be "small volumes" with a "very disciplined approach"; CFO Patel: beyond the staffing reduction, CCI aims to buy land under towers and invest in automation, saying by 2030 it can deliver "well over 200 basis points in margin improvements."

  • Nicholas Del Deo, MoffettNathanson: asked about land ownership targets and CapEx trajectory; CFO Patel: "our goal is over the next handful of years, get to a point where we own from 30% to as much as 40% of the land underneath our towers" and "this year's guide should be adequate."

  • Brandon Nispel, KeyBanc: challenged the dividend payout level and suggested shifting to buybacks; CFO Patel: the company decided "to basically reiterate that the dividend would stay where it is now" while paying down debt to "remain investment grade" and executing "$1 billion of shares" repurchases.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to pressing around execution and capital allocation, with repeated probing on closing timing, dividend policy, and potential technology threats (e.g., fixed wireless vs satellite, fiber-to-the-home), including KeyBanc’s framing that a dividend cut could be "more accretive" alongside buybacks.

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive and steady in prepared remarks and generally confident in answers, repeatedly using firm language on key items such as closing confidence ("extremely confident") and DISH enforcement ("We believe we have a strong legal case against DISH" (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)), while avoiding precise timing on uncertain items like litigation outcomes ("legal outcome is going to take at least a year" (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)).

  • Versus last quarter, management maintained the same core messages (asset-sale timing, DISH enforcement, and cost actions) but added more detail on the DISH litigation posture (including "a claim against EchoStar") and framed edge compute explicitly as a "trial phase." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company’s strategic priorities were consistent quarter over quarter: completing the small cell and fiber sale in 1H 2026, defending value from the DISH agreement, and reshaping the organization to run a U.S.-only tower business.

  • New in the current quarter was added specificity on legal escalation: management said it "amended" the DISH litigation to include "breach of contract" and a claim involving EchoStar, versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on seeking to recover "in excess of $3.5 billion" and supporting spectrum being put to work while enforcing contract rights.

  • The tone in analyst questioning broadened from Q4’s concentration on DISH mechanics and transaction approvals to Q1’s heavier focus on medium-term growth drivers (5G densification, 2027+ spectrum auctions, edge compute monetization), plus more pointed dividend framework debate.

Risks and Concerns

  • "We are seeking to recover the remaining payments DISH owed" and management cautioned on uncertainty and duration, stating "legal outcome is going to take at least a year" and timing "is still very much an unknown." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

  • Management highlighted cost timing risk in quarterly results: Q1 cost benefits were "largely due to timing and seasonality" and are expected later in the year. (Executive VP & CFO Patel)

  • On potential technology substitution, management repeatedly framed satellite as complementary and not currently material to the tower business, saying impact is "de minimis or inconsequential at this point." (CEO, President & Director Hillabrant)

Final Takeaway

Management reiterated full-year 2026 guidance while positioning 2026 as a transition to a “stand-alone tower business,” anchored by closing the small cell and fiber sale in 1H 2026, enforcing claims tied to DISH’s default (including updated litigation that adds breach of contract claims and allegations involving EchoStar), and implementing efficiency actions tied to a $65 million annualized run-rate cost reduction. Management also kept its post-close capital framework intact—planning about $1 billion of share repurchases and about $7 billion of debt repayment—to support its stated leverage target and investment-grade profile, while describing newer growth-adjacent initiatives like edge compute as being in an early “trial phase.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Brent crude tops $100 as Iran attacks three ships, U.S. crude stocks post surprise draw
data.description:
Crude oil futures jumped Wednesday as the Strait of Hormuz remained closed, with Iran's Revolutionary Guard saying it fired on three vessels and seized two in the strait, and the latest weekly data showed a surprise draw in U.S. crude stockpiles.

Iran's parliament speaker and top negotiator Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf said a reopening of the strait was impossible while the U.S. continues its naval blockade of the country's ports, which he called "a flagrant ​breach of the ceasefire."

Reuters reported the U.S. military intercepted at least three Iranian-flagged tankers in ‌Asian waters and is redirecting them away from their positions near India, Malaysia and Sri Lanka.

Analysts said President Trump's extension of the ceasefire to give Iran more time to present a proposal for peace talks is seen as extending the time it takes for the strait to reopen.

"The lack of a peace deal or any plan moving forward has left the Strait of Hormuz and all energy assets in the Persian Gulf in a kind of limbo," Mizuho analyst Robert Yawger said in a note.

Trump's extension removes the immediate prospect of escalation, but delays talks "and likely keeps the U.S. blockade intact indefinitely," Ritterbusch analysts wrote, adding that "price-related demand destruction is becoming an increased topic of conversation."

The U.S. Energy Information Administration reported crude oil inventories rose last week as imports picked up from the week before and exports declined.

Commercial crude oil stocks excluding the Strategic Petroleum Reserve rose by 1.9M barrels to 465.7M barrels in the week ended April 17 and 3% above the five-year average for the time of year, the EIA said; crude stocks were expected to have fallen by ~1M barrels.

Gasoline inventories fell by a larger-than-expected 4.6M barrels at 228.4M and were 0.5% below the five-year average, the EIA said, and distillate fuel stocks also fell more than expected, down by 3.4M barrels to 108.1M barrels and 8% below the five-year average for the time of year.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for June delivery jumped 3.7% to $92.96/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery climbed 3.5% to $101.91/bbl, with both benchmarks rising for the third straight day.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) gained for the sixth consecutive session, although gains are small in the low-demand spring season; the front-month Nymex May contract rose 0.9% to $2.722/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: CSX anticipates 2026 free cash flow growth of more than 60% as revenue outlook moves to mid-single digits
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CSX Corporation (CSX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We now expect full year revenue growth in the mid-single digits versus low single digits previously,” said (CEO Stephen Angel), tying the change “largely” to “higher-than-expected energy prices, particularly diesel,” which he said will start lifting fuel-related revenue in Q2.
  • Angel reiterated profitability and cash priorities, saying, “We will anticipate year-over-year operating margin expansion of 200 to 300 basis points, but we now expect results to trend toward the high end of that range,” while keeping “total 2026 capital spending… below $2.4 billion” and adding, “We now anticipate free cash flow to grow by more than 60% compared to 2025.”
  • “Total revenue increased 2% on 3% volume growth… Total expenses fell by 6%… operating income increased 20% with earnings per share up 26%,” said (Executive VP & CFO Kevin Boone), adding that the company is seeing “over $100 million of year-over-year efficiency savings” within a $153 million year-over-year expense decline.
  • “Our FRA injury rate improved by 13%… and our train accident rate improved by over 30%,” said (Executive VP & COO Michael Cory), while highlighting “record first quarter fuel efficiency of 0.97 gallons per 1,000 gross ton miles.”

Outlook

  • Angel said the 2026 revenue outlook change is “largely driven by higher-than-expected energy prices, particularly diesel,” and emphasized that while “higher fuel increases our revenue and expand our expenses, which can pressure reported margin,” CSX is “pleased with our cost performance year-to-date.”
  • Angel kept the operating margin expansion framework at “200 to 300 basis points,” but shifted expectation to “trend toward the high end of that range,” alongside reiterated “below $2.4 billion” 2026 capex and “more than 60%” 2026 free cash flow growth vs. 2025.
  • Compared with Q4 2025 guidance, CSX raised the full-year revenue growth view (from “low single-digit revenue growth” in the prior call to “mid-single digits” in Q1 2026) and increased the free cash flow growth target (from “at least 50%” to “more than 60%,” both vs. 2025).

Financial Results

  • Boone reported “total revenue increased 2% on 3% volume growth,” with “pricing gains and higher fuel recovery… offset by business mix impacts,” and said “total expenses fell by 6%,” driving “operating income increased 20%” and “earnings per share up 26%.”
  • Boone said first-quarter expense “decreased by $153 million” year-over-year, including “over $100 million of year-over-year efficiency savings,” with offsets from “inflation and higher fuel prices.” He added labor costs were “1% lower” as a “5% reduction in headcount” and a “$10 million reduction in overtime expense” offset inflation.
  • Boone flagged items affecting sequential comparisons, stating, “we do expect some nonseasonal expense from incentive compensation, timing of contractual locomotive costs, including overhauls and advisory costs related to industry consolidation,” and later clarified Q2 will not repeat “the real estate gain that occurred in the first quarter of $44 million.”

Q&A

  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: Asked for productivity “numbers” and how CSX sustains the “high end” of the 200–300 bps margin expansion range with fuel headwinds; (CFO Boone) said the plan has “over 100 different initiatives,” with early delivery “more quickly than we thought,” and added “energy… vehicle spend… utilities” are “in the crosshairs.”
  • Ken Hoexter, BofA: Asked timing/scalability of Howard Street Tunnel double-stack and volume ramp; (SVP & CCO Maryclare Kenney) said “The last bridge should be complete in the next week or so, and then we will have double stack access,” adding it “take[s] about a day out of our current transit” and “new services typically takes a couple of bid seasons” to fully ramp.
  • Scott Group, Wolfe: Asked PS&O sustainability and Q1–Q2 margin noise; (CFO Boone) said “we're never done there,” cited procurement/vendor work, and pointed to Q2 items including no repeat of the “$44 million” gain, higher “engine… overhauls,” “transaction cost,” and higher fuel pressure.
  • Brian Ossenbeck, JPMorgan: Asked about the real estate gain run-rate and elevated dwell; (CFO Boone) said no other gains “of this size” are planned, while (COO Cory) said some delays reflect new work methods and curfew execution, adding, “we're all very aware of the dwell and the train speed… and we'll bring that back in line.”
  • Brandon Oglenski, Barclays: Asked how CSX drives higher ROIC; (CEO Angel) said the “best way” is improving the numerator via “operating margins” and productivity, while capital discipline requires “every project now… stand on its own” and “predictive analytics” to prioritize spend.
  • Thomas Wadewitz, UBS: Asked pricing lag from truck tightening; (CCO Kenney) said same-store pricing should be “better this year than what we saw last year,” noted only “50 or so percent” of the book can be repriced annually, and said international intermodal is “primarily contracted under long-term deals.”
  • Ariel Rosa, Citi: Asked vulnerability from potential industry consolidation; (CEO Angel) said any merger is “a long process,” and CSX will “focus on execution… from a position of strength.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative (analysts): Questions concentrated on fuel-driven margin pressure, sustainability of PS&O savings, service metrics (dwell), and strategic risk from consolidation, including “Any thoughts on how to think about sort of sequential margin improvement” (Scott Group, Wolfe) and consolidation “vulnerabilities” (Ariel Rosa, Citi).
  • Slightly positive (management): Prepared remarks and answers emphasized control of costs and confidence in execution, including “2026 is off to a strong start” (CFO Kevin Boone) and “we remain focused on our goals and are confident in our ability to continue this momentum” (CEO Stephen Angel).
  • Versus the prior quarter: Management language moved from “a challenging year” and “soft” markets (Q4 2025) to “strong start” with higher top-line guidance largely from fuel (Q1 2026), while analyst tone shifted from restructuring/OR-bridge questions toward durability of savings, operational trade-offs, and timing of growth unlocks (Howard Street Tunnel).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance changed from Q4’s “low single-digit revenue growth” assumption to Q1’s “mid-single digits,” with Angel explicitly attributing the change to diesel-driven fuel revenue starting in Q2.
  • Free cash flow growth target increased from “at least 50%” (Q4 2025) to “more than 60%” (Q1 2026), while 2026 capex remained “below $2.4 billion” and operating margin expansion stayed “200 to 300 basis points,” now “toward the high end.”
  • Strategic emphasis evolved from Q4’s reset (Angel: “I am replacing our 2025, 2027 targets with the guidance we've given for 2026 only”) to Q1’s focus on sustaining a cost “muscle” and building a “pipeline” for 2027 initiatives (Boone).

Risks and Concerns

  • Kenney cautioned that “energy cost inflation poses risk to consumer demand and imports,” while also saying higher diesel is creating “tailwinds for freight conversions.”
  • Kenney said “housing affordability remains a real headwind,” with “additional closures year-to-date” in forest products, and added “Automotive continues to be pressured by lower production and the extended retooling of a major plant on our network.”
  • Boone highlighted Q2 cost noise from “incentive compensation,” “contractual locomotive costs, including overhauls,” and “advisory costs related to industry consolidation,” while Cory acknowledged productivity initiatives can temporarily “cause delay” and require capital fixes (e.g., yards/sidings).

Final Takeaway

CSX management described Q1 2026 as a strong operational and cost start, with year-over-year expense reductions and efficiency programs supporting higher operating income and EPS, while raising full-year revenue growth expectations to mid-single digits largely from diesel-linked fuel recovery. Angel kept the 200–300 bps operating margin expansion framework but guided toward the high end, reiterated capex below $2.4 billion, and increased the 2026 free cash flow growth target to more than 60% versus 2025, while executives flagged Q2 nonseasonal costs, fuel-driven margin pressure mechanics, and macro risks in housing, automotive, and import demand.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: YPF investors to pursue international treaty arbitration in $16B judgment
data.description:
YPF (YPF) investors who saw their $16B U.S. judgment against Argentina thrown out on appeal last month have told the trial judge that they plan to pursue arbitration while continuing to contest the appeals court ruling, Bloomberg reported Wednesday.

A U.S. District Court awarded the plaintiffs the judgment in 2023, ruling that Argentina's government violated their rights in its 2012 nationalization of oil company YPF (YPF), but the U.S. Second Circuit Court of Appeals reversed the judgment on March 27, saying she misinterpreted Argentine law.

Litigation funder Burford Capital (BUR), which has been financing the lawsuit and would have taken a large share of the award, said after the ruling it could take the case to arbitration as well as appeal the case to the full Second Circuit or the U.S. Supreme Court.

Burford (BUR) shares plunged after last month's ruling and have since lost ~40%.

data.title: Amerisafe signals mid-single-digit premium growth amid soft pricing and rate pressure
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AMERISAFE, Inc. (AMSF) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We are pleased with our solid start to 2026, marked by continued growth, disciplined execution and attractive underwriting performance." (President, CEO, Principal Accounting & Financial Officer and Director G. Frost)
  • "During the quarter, we grew net premiums earned by 9%. We also delivered a combined ratio of 93.2% and produced operating earnings of $0.50 per share." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "The workers' compensation market remains competitive and continues to operate in a prolonged soft pricing environment amid persistent industry headwinds, such as claims severity and economic uncertainty." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "Retention for policies for which we offered renewal was 92.4% in the quarter, and pricing remains strong, helping offset continued downward pressure in filed loss costs." (Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Vincent Gagliano)
  • "Net investment income decreased 0.8% to $6.6 million due to lower average investable assets." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "Next quarter, I'll have the pleasure of passing the financial remarks off to Guillermo Ramos, our new CFO." (President and CEO Frost)

Outlook

  • "We continue to see positive wage growth in our targeted classes of business, coming in at 4.5% for the quarter, while headcount change was essentially flat." (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • "We believe continued payroll growth across our targeted industries indicates relatively healthy business activity despite ongoing economic uncertainty." (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • "We're still looking at mid-single-digit decreases for the year" on loss costs, with filings in AMERISAFE’s five biggest states ranging "from down almost 9% to down 1.2%." (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • "Is it linear? No. But we're shooting for that mid-single-digit range, and we've been hitting that." (President and CEO Frost)

Financial Results

  • "Gross premiums written were $88.5 million" and "net earned premiums were $75.1 million in the quarter." (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • "The current accident year loss ratio was 72% for the quarter" and "we had $7.6 million or 10.1 points of favorable development in the quarter," resulting in "a net loss ratio of 61.9% for the quarter." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "Total underwriting and other expenses were $22.3 million for the quarter, resulting in an expense ratio of 29.7%." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "Net income was $8.1 million or $0.43 per diluted share, while operating net income was $9.5 million or $0.50 per diluted share." (President and CEO Frost)
  • "We repurchased nearly 120,000 shares of common stock" for "a total of $4 million" and said remaining authorization was "$12.9 million." (President and CEO Frost)

Q&A

  • Mark Hughes, Truist Securities: "how did you see inflation in the quarter?"; President and CEO Frost: "No marginal changes from what we talked about at year-end, Mark. Medical inflation is real. We are living it. We are reserving properly for it."
  • Mark Hughes, Truist Securities: "How about NCCI loss costs"; Executive VP Gagliano: "We're still looking at mid-single-digit decreases for the year" and cited a range "from down almost 9% to down 1.2%."
  • David Samar, Citizens JMP: "are there any certain industries or areas of the market that are driving growth"; President and CEO Frost: "it's been pretty steady across our book of business" and "it's been pretty prolific throughout the book of business."
  • Robert Edward Farnam, Brean Capital: "how does that compare to the duration of your liabilities?"; President and CEO Frost: "our average duration on our portfolio -- on our liabilities is between 3 and 4 years" and attributed it to "our high-touch model" to "close and settle these claims as quickly as we can."
  • Mark Hughes, Truist Securities: "how sustainable" is growth; President and CEO Frost: "I totally believe it is sustainable" and "we're shooting for that mid-single-digit range."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative (analysts) with inflation, loss-cost decreases, and industry deterioration in focus, including "when you look at... accident years '24 and '25" and "pressure" questions around trend durability. (Mark Hughes, Truist; Robert Edward Farnam, Brean)
  • Neutral to slightly positive (management) with confidence anchored on pricing/risk selection, including "We remain confident in our strategy" and "committed to consistent execution." (President and CEO Frost)
  • Compared with last quarter, management again emphasized soft-market pressure, but Q1 discussion added a near-term positive data point: "no claims with incurred value over $1 million" in the current accident year quarter. (President and CEO Frost)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current-quarter growth pace moderated versus Q4: "$88.5 million" gross premiums written in Q1 2026 vs. "gross premium written grew 11.7%" in Q4 2025. (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • Retention eased: "92.4%" in Q1 2026 vs. "93.7%" in Q4 2025. (Executive VP Gagliano)
  • Large-claim commentary shifted from Q4 severity discussion ("25 claims with incurred value over $1 million") to Q1 quarter commentary of "no claims with incurred value over $1 million" in the quarter. (President and CEO Frost)
  • Capital and portfolio metrics were updated with a smaller cash and invested assets base: "approximately $774 million" at Q1 2026 quarter end vs. "roughly $797 million" at Q4 2025 quarter end. (President and CEO Frost)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Prolonged soft pricing environment" and "downward pressure in filed loss costs" were framed as ongoing constraints. (President and CEO Frost; Executive VP Gagliano)
  • "Continued rate pressure and general high claim severity" were cited as creating "modest upward pressure on the current accident year" loss ratio. (President and CEO Frost)
  • Medical inflation remained a key theme: "Medical inflation is real" and "continued pressure on medical inflation industry-wide." (President and CEO Frost)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a continuation of disciplined, distribution-led premium expansion in a soft pricing market, pairing 9% net earned premium growth with a 93.2% combined ratio, while highlighting persistent rate/loss-cost pressure and medical severity inflation; leadership also signaled a CFO transition next quarter, continued share repurchases, and reiterated that growth is expected to remain “mid-single-digit” and “sustainable,” though not linear.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Northrop Grumman Systems wins $207M contract modification, lifting the value to $596M
data.description:
• Northrop Grumman Systems (NOC) has received a $207.9M contract modification for one more year of logistics support services.
  • With this move, the total contract value has increased from $388.1M to $596.0M.
  • The work under this contract is expected to continue until April 30, 2027.
  • At the time of award, $78.0M in FMS funds have been allocated, with the Air Force Life Cycle Management Center handling the contract.
  • On April 22, the stock closed slightly lower at ~$588.52 in after-hours.

data.title: General Dynamics unit wins $230M U.S. Navy contract modification
data.description:
• General Dynamics (GD) unit Bath Iron Works won a $230.5M contract modification for work on Arleigh Burke-class guided missile destroyers.
  • The contract covers planning, yard services, long-lead material support, and procurement of key ship components.
  • The work will be carried out in Bath, Maine, and is scheduled to be completed by July 2027.
  • If all contract options are fully exercised, the total contract value could reach $895M.
  • At the time of award, about $10.2M (4%) in Navy FY26 funds will be obligated, which will expire at the end of FY26.
  • The Supervisor of Shipbuilding, Conversion, and Repair, Bath, Maine, is the contracting activity.

data.title: Weatherford says Mexico starting to pay suppliers as oilfield activity normalizes
data.description:
Weatherford International (WFRD) said Wednesday that oilfield activity in Mexico has normalized and the company is starting to get paid ​after Pemex raised funds to pay its suppliers after long delays, Reuters reported.

Oilfield service companies had significantly reduced activities due to state-run Pemex's accumulation of billions of dollars in pending payments to ​contractors and providers in recent years.

"We are starting to get paid," and "we think that stability will continue on an activity level... So I ​think over the next few years, [Mexico] will be a bright spot," Weatherford (WFRD) CEO Girish Saligram said on the company's earnings conference call.

Weatherford (WFRD) received a large payment in Q4 2025 and another in Q1 from its main customer in Mexico, which still owed $283M as of March 31, CFO Anuj Dhruv said on the call, without identifying the customer by name.

The company also warned a hit to earnings from the Middle East will get worse this quarter, totaling $30M-$50M over this year's H1, Saligram said, based on oilfield activity in the region starting to normalize by the end of the current quarter.

Weatherford (WFRD) has been forced to pause operations for several weeks in countries including Iraq, Qatar, and parts of Kuwait, the CEO said, adding that a jump in freight costs, jet fuel, and trucking expenses also have caused strain.

Impacts from the war will show more clearly in Q2 after the company reported better-than-expected Q1 earnings, Saligram said, while noting a resolution to the conflict would mean oilfield services companies would be among the first to reap the benefits as countries rebuild their damaged energy infrastructure.
